misconceptionSummary: Students think 10⁻³ means moving the decimal 3 places to the right instead of the left.

steps.narration: Each step down divides by ten. From one — ten to the zero — dividing by ten again gives one tenth, then one hundredth, then one thousandth. The pattern continues smoothly through zero.

visuals.ruleText: For 10⁻ⁿ, the absolute value of the exponent equals the number of decimal places
visuals.subText: 10⁻³ = 0.001 — three decimal places, digit 1 in the thousandths column

steps.narration: The size of the negative exponent tells you exactly how many zeros appear after the decimal point before the one. Ten to the minus three has three decimal places.
Going the other way: count the decimal places to find the exponent. Zero point zero zero zero zero one has five decimal places, so it equals ten to the minus five.

visuals.label: Negative exponent → count decimal places before the 1

steps.narration: Ten to the minus four is zero point zero zero zero one. Four decimal places, digit one in the ten-thousandths column.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking 10⁻³ = 1000 because the negative 'cancels out'. Negative powers make fractions — 10⁻³ = 0.001, which is one thousandth.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: A negative exponent does not cancel to give a large positive number. It means a fraction — divide 1 by the positive power. 10⁻³ = 1 ÷ 1000 = 0.001.
